sub_entries.definition: (pf) to cover as with a sash

citations.text: 29.5: mukhanya sinabuknya riṅ sari nikaṅ kusuma surabhi maṅlare hati. In Bali the sabuk is now an item of female ceremonial attire, a strip of fine material wound around the torso from breast to waist, covering the bulaṅ. In the kidungs, however, the sabuk is always an item of male dress, and corresponds to the modern bal. saput (high: kampuh), a piece of fine cloth worn on ceremonial occasions over the kambĕn (wastra). In ModJ. and Malay the sabuk is mainly a male garment.

definition: (Skt born together, congenital, innate, hereditary, original, natural). A difficult word. In the prose texts found only in Wir (4x) and Udy; in the kak. very frequently; in the kid. seldom. The bal. com. use especially wyakti, kadi (also tuwi). In bal. pasaja means ``really, very'' (e.g. uttama pasaja as rendering of lĕwih tĕmĕn). In the examples which follow an exhaustive survey of the use of sahaja is given. 1. of (its) own accord, spontaneously, naturally 2. as a matter of course, with the natural consequence that, naturally, certainly (wyakti, niyata), without further ado, and straight away 3. it is clear, apparently, you can see that, one would say that, as it were. Sometimes a translation such as ``but then'', ``lo and behold!'', ``and even'', ``on top of that'' (closer to sahaja 1. ``spontaneously'', ``suddenly'') might be preferable.

definition: (Skt sahya, to be endured, tolerable; able to bear, equal to; powerful, strong; sweet, agreeable; help, assistance; sāhya, conquering, victory, aid, assistance, conjunction, society (in this meaning perhaps from saha or contracted from sāhāyya). In OJ the meaning of sahya appears to be ``powerful'', ``strong'', ``vigorous'', but also ``enduring'', ``lasting'', ``constantly'' (coming close to sahiṣṇu, q.v.), and perhaps ``fine'', ``elegant'', ``beautiful'' (cf Skt ``sweet, agreeable'', and ModJ sae). Occasionally the possibility of sahya = saha cannot be ruled out. See also apra-, pra-.

81.33: mwaṅ hana tâmuput salaga wāni pamaja nṛpati. ``Bud'' seems more acceptable than ``calyx'' (the ModJ slaga) in view of the comparisons involving waiting for the rain; also a young woman is compared to a salaga (Sum 65.1; 65.2). In combinations such as asana salaga, it is better to take salaga (``in bud'') rather than as a hybrid compound. The bal. com. render salaga with mĕnur, mlaṭi and pusuh (``closed bud''). Salaga niṅ mĕnur (Sum 164.3), however, shows that salaga and mĕnur are not the same.

definition: 1. (Skt) core, pith or solid interior of anything; essential, chief part. See also kṛṣṇa-. 2. (Skt) firmness, strength, power, energy. See also alpa-, anupama-, ati-, nis-, prati-, su-. 3. firm, strong, powerful, energetic; having the power to, capable of, fit to be trusted with. Tan sāra, see under asāra (below).

spells sāwadāna, AbhW sawādāna, sawāda#na. It is used for rendering anurūpa (Utt and perh.  BhP) and may be derived via Skt sam#mukha, ``facing, opposite; fit, suitable''. Cf Skt saṃwadana, speaking together (see var. Utt 7.23 below), and saṃwāda, concurrence, agreement, conformity. Skt sāwadhāna, ``having attention, careful, heedful'', does not seem to make sense.

sub_entries.definition: to one's heart's content; every desire

citations.text: 5.15: amet lalab sakasĕnĕṅ;

8.7: anuwuki sakasĕnĕṅ niṅ indriya. Note that (1) in sakasĕnĕṅ the meaning is not ``loved one'', but at the most ``that to which one's longing goes forth''; (2) the sĕnĕṅ is always a loved one who is absent, is about to depart or has just returned (the word is not used in the usual amorous conversations), hence ``the long desired one''; (3) the word is found only in kakawins.

2. certain ceremonial objects (esp. in a wedding-ceremony or the consecration of a king) which represent and are substitutes for a person (e.g. a kris as substitute for the bride? see Mal 8.119b, s.v. sĕlaṅ); also: the place where these objects are arranged and the ceremony takes place (Ww 3.83a: puja niṅ saṅ dwija muṅgw iṅ pasilih;  CSt+ 3.69a: sri bupati ... aliṅgih iṅ pasĕlaṅ, s.v. sĕlaṅ). definition: sharp, sharpened (by filing, grinding); also of finished feminine beauty or manly courage in its freshness of being ``newly sharpened'' (esp. dug siṅī). Used with daluwaṅ and gula (madhu) it seems to point to a process they have been subjected to, in the latter case prob. pinastika (see pastika). Cf bal. saṅih?

definition: The problem is, how to explain that both sipi and tan sipi can mean ``very, to a high degree''. The use of sipi in RY, where it occurs in the description of a battle and apparently in contradistinction to kapisan, may suggest a solution. Sipi: not completely, only half, not directly (fatal). Tan sipi: completely, not by halves, thorough, utter, total. The fact that sipi is often followed by kari, karika could then point to its use by way of an ironical or rhetorical question: ``Is it only half?'', meaning the opposite: ``fully, completely''. In this way sipi may have acquired the meaning of ``utter, total, in the highest degree'' (= tan sipi). Sipi and tan sipi in RY:

sub_entries.definition: utter, total, very great, very much, in the highest degree, complete

definition: (Skt minute, small, fine; atomic, intangible; acute, subtle, keen). See also ādi-, anta-, ati-, catur-, dewa-, jalada-, megha-, parama-, paramārtha-, su-. 1. subtle, of subtle matter, ethereal, unsubstantial, i.e. not accessible to the usual organs of perception, but perceptible to those gifted with supernatural powers; invisible, immaterial (hence comparable to śūnya, niṣkala, q.v.; it is the state of the deity not manifesting itself in visible form). Thus it be#comes: divine (supernatural being), the supernatural manifestation from another world; the invisible essence of what is sensually perceptible; (being present as) the essence of 2. to assume an invisible state, disappear 3. subtle, sly, wily, devious, insidious, underhand, secret; wile, trick, device etc

sub_entries.definition: 1. to be present invisibly, to be in an immaterial condition, to make os. invisible 2. to enter unseen into, conceal os. in, disappear into 3. sinūkṣma (acting)secretly, in secret; (tr.) to do st. secretly, undertake in a secret or deceitful way 5. to enter st. as its invisible essence, i.e. to incarnate os. in; to assume the outward form of > as it were ...
